studies.title: Studies
studies.subtitle: Quick summaries, practical tips and useful sources to review nursing every day.
studies.communityDesc: Coming soon: access to a group for summaries, materials and new content alerts.

procedures.title: Procedures
procedures.description: Essential steps before, during and after.

content.vitals_1: BP, HR, RR, temperature, pain and SpO₂ should be assessed together, not in isolation.
content.vitals_2: Sudden changes or worsening trends require reassessment and quick team communication.
content.vitals_3: Record time, context, action taken and patient response.
content.medications_1: Check patient, medication, dose, route, time, dilution and record before administration.
content.medications_2: Allergies, interactions and duplicate therapies must be checked before medication.
content.medications_3: If dose or prescription is unclear, pause and confirm with the responsible team.
content.safety_1: Hand hygiene is the main barrier against healthcare-associated infections.
content.safety_2: Patient identification must happen before medications, collections and procedures.
content.safety_3: Fall risk, pressure injury risk and allergies should be visible in the care plan.
content.procedures_1: Before: explain, confirm identity, gather supplies and preserve privacy.
content.procedures_2: During: maintain proper technique, observe discomfort and report incidents.
content.procedures_3: After: dispose supplies, document findings and monitor clinical response.

quickTips.tip_1: Use mind maps to review anatomy, pharmacology and clinical assessment.
quickTips.tip_2: Practice dose and drip calculations with short daily examples.
quickTips.tip_3: Turn protocols into checklists to memorize critical steps.
quickTips.tip_4: Review warning signs by system: respiratory, cardiovascular, neurological and metabolic.

dailyTips.title: Study tip of the day
items: Review one normal vital sign range and one warning sign before your next shift.
When studying medications, link class, indication, adverse effects and nursing care.
Practice explaining a procedure in simple words as if guiding a patient.
Create small safety checklists to turn theory into practical routine.
Spend 10 minutes reviewing common abbreviations: BP, HR, RR, SpO₂, IM, IV, SC and PO.
When studying a topic, ask: what do I observe, what do I document and when do I escalate?
Practice one drip-rate calculation to keep your math agile.
Review hypo- and hyperglycemia by comparing symptoms and initial actions.
Make flashcards for administration routes and common sites.
Review standard precautions: hand hygiene, PPE, disposal and exposure prevention.
Read a short protocol and turn the main steps into three bullets.
Practice clinical reasoning: abnormal finding + possible cause + first safe action.

vitals.intro: A foundation for assessing clinical stability and early deterioration.

sections.title: What to observe
items: Blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate, temperature, pain and SpO₂.
Compare current values with history, diagnosis, medications and complaints.
Observe associated signs: sweating, pallor, dyspnea, confusion, drowsiness or intense pain.

sections.title: Warning points
items: Low SpO₂, altered consciousness, hypotension, persistent tachycardia or high fever need prompt attention.
A worsening trend can matter more than one isolated value.
Reassess after intervention and document clinical response.

sections.title: Remember
items: Always document time, patient position, oxygen device and action taken.
Vital signs are objective data but must be interpreted with clinical context.

medications.intro: Safety summary for medication administration and review.

sections.title: Before administration
items: Confirm patient, medication, dose, route, time, dilution, expiry and prescription.
Check allergies, interactions, duplicates and required parameters before the dose.
If prescription or dose is unclear, stop and confirm before administration.

sections.title: During and after
items: Watch for immediate reactions, pain, extravasation, nausea, dizziness or allergic signs.
Document administration, time, route, site when applicable and incidents.
High-alert medications require double-checking according to institutional protocol.

sections.title: To study
items: Review common classes: analgesics, antibiotics, anticoagulants, insulins and antihypertensives.
Link each class to key nursing care points.

safety.intro: Best practices to reduce risk and protect patient, student and team.

sections.title: Priorities
items: Correct identification before any care, collection, medication or procedure.
Hand hygiene at indicated moments and proper PPE use.
Safe environment: call bell reachable, rails per risk, dry floor and locked bed.

sections.title: Common risks
items: Falls, pressure injury, allergies, infection, medication error and communication failure.
Use objective handoff with situation, background, assessment and recommendation.
Report incidents and near misses to improve processes.

sections.title: Remember
items: Safety starts before technique: confirm, prepare, communicate and document.
A simple checklist reduces omissions in repetitive routines.

procedures.intro: Quick structure to study and perform procedures safely.

sections.title: Before
items: Read the order or indication, confirm identity, explain the procedure and gather supplies.
Assess contraindications, allergies, pain, privacy and need for help.
Organize the environment to avoid interruptions and contamination.

sections.title: During
items: Maintain clean or sterile technique according to the procedure.
Watch for discomfort, bleeding, vital sign changes or unexpected reactions.
Communicate incidents and ask for support when risk appears.

sections.title: After
items: Dispose supplies correctly, perform hand hygiene and leave the patient safe.
Document procedure, findings, supplies used and patient response.
Monitor expected effects and signs of complications.
